coredata.dc:title: BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst co-modified with N-doped graphene nanodots and Ag nanoparticles for improved photocatalytic oxidation: A significant enhancement in photoinduced carrier separation and broad-spectrum light absorption
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Changchang
Din, Syed Taj Ud
Seo, Won Cheol
Lee, Jeongwoo
Kim, Youjoong
Jung, Hyun
Yang, Woochul
coredata.dc:description: The efficient removal of antibiotics in water is essential to protect aquatic environment as antibiotics are some of the most hazardous pollutants in water. Herein, we report the synthesis of a novel N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst consisting of BiVO4 coupled with Ag nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) and nitrogen-doped graphene nanodots (N-GNDs) to degrade and mineralize tetracycline hydrochloride (TC•HCl) in water. The ternary photocatalyst shows higher photocatalytic degradation of TC•HCl under full spectrum light than pure BiVO4. Based on the morphology, crystalline structure, physicochemical properties, and photoelectrochemical behavior of the ternary photocatalyst, the enhanced photocatalytic performance is attributed to the upconversion effect of N-GNDs and the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect of Ag-NPs. Thus, the synergetic effects expand the absorption range of light and significantly promotes the separation of photoexcited carriers. Further, a possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of TC•HCl degradation over N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 is discussed via through investigation of the charge carrier migration, active species, and intermediates in the degradation processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4
Upconversion effect
Localized surface plasmon resonance effect
Ternary photocatalyst
Tetracycline hydrochloride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of the as-prepared photocatalysts
Photocatalytic degradation of TC•HCl using the synthesized photocatalysts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of pure BiVO4 and NAB-4 (a) and magnified peaks (220) (b).
SEM images of BiVO4 (a), Ag/BiVO4 (b) and NAB-4 (c); TEM images of NAB-4 (d, e) and EDS analysis of NAB-4 with weights of each component (f).
XPS high-resolution spectra of C 1s (a), Bi 4f (b), V 2p (c), O 1s (d) in BiVO4 and NAB-4 and that of N 1s (e) and Ag 3d (f) in NAB-4.

Degradation dynamic curves of TC•HCl solutions over BiVO4, Ag/BiVO4, NAB-4 (a) and N-GQDs/Ag/BiVO4 with different Ag contents (NAB-1, -2, -3, -4, and -5) (b). ln(C0/C) versus time (c), and absorption spectra of TC•HCl over NAB-4 (d).
Cyclic degradation kinetic curves of TC•HCl (a) and NAB-4 XRD patterns before and after five successive photocatalytic reactions (b).
UV–vis diffuses reflectance spectra of BiVO4, 10.8% Ag/BiVO4 and NAB-4.
Transient photocurrent response (a) and EIS (b) of pure BiVO4, Ag/BiVO4 and NAB-4.
PL emission spectra (a) and time-resolved fluorescence decay curves (b) of pure BiVO4, Ag/BiVO4, and NAB-4.

Active species trapping experiment (a), ESR spectra of NAB-4 in methanol dispersion for DMPO-
O2 – (b) and in aqueous dispersion for DMPO-
OH (c).
MS of TC•HCl over NAB-4 at 0 (a), 40 (b) and 80 min (c).
Possible reaction intermediates in the TC•HCl degradation process over NAB-4.
Possible migration and separation of carriers behavior and photocatalytic degradation mechanism over N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4.

head.ce:title: BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst co-modified with N-doped graphene nanodots and Ag nanoparticles for improved photocatalytic oxidation: A significant enhancement in photoinduced carrier separation and broad-spectrum light absorption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institue of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institue of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institue of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, Republic of Korea (C. Ma).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficient removal of antibiotics in water is essential to protect aquatic environment as antibiotics are some of the most hazardous pollutants in water. Herein, we report the synthesis of a novel N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst consisting of BiVO4 coupled with Ag nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) and nitrogen-doped graphene nanodots (N-GNDs) to degrade and mineralize tetracycline hydrochloride (TC•HCl) in water. The ternary photocatalyst shows higher photocatalytic degradation of TC•HCl under full spectrum light than pure BiVO4. Based on the morphology, crystalline structure, physicochemical properties, and photoelectrochemical behavior of the ternary photocatalyst, the enhanced photocatalytic performance is attributed to the upconversion effect of N-GNDs and the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect of Ag-NPs. Thus, the synergetic effects expand the absorption range of light and significantly promotes the separation of photoexcited carriers. Further, a possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of TC•HCl degradation over N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 is discussed via through investigation of the charge carrier migration, active species, and intermediates in the degradation processes.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, water pollution has become an increasingly serious environmental hazard, restricting sustainable growth and development [1,2]. Tetracycline hydrochloride as a typical antibiotic widely used in human and veterinary medicine, and in aquaculture. After the drug is metabolized in the body, the toxic byproduct enters the environment, and eventually damages the ecosystem and human health [3]. Various techniques, such as adsorption, microbial decomposition, and photocatalysis, have been employed to address water pollution [4,5]. Photocatalysis is a green and effective strategy due to its high efficiency and low cost. Therefore, diverse photocatalysts such as TiO2 [6], ZnO [7], CdS [8], and BiOX [9] have been developed for the degradation of pollutants. TiO2 has been considered as a potential photocatalyst because of its outstanding oxidative ability, cost-effectiveness as well as physical and chemical stability in a variety of environments. However, as TiO2 responds only to ultraviolet light, its utilization of solar energy is limited, and thus its practical applications are restricted [10]. Thus, the design of novel photocatalysts with an outstanding response to broad-spectrum light is essential for the efficient removal of environmental pollutants.

Recently, BiVO4 has attracted a great deal of attention due to its superior band structure, outstanding photo-response, relatively high photostability and potential photocatalytic applications in oxidation and degradation of hazardous organic pollutants in water systems. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance of pure BiVO4 is insufficient for practical applications because of its poor visible (Vis) light absorption efficiency and serious recombination rate of photoexcited charge [11]. The construction of heterojunction was proposed as an efficient strategy to address the drawbacks of BiVO4. For instance, the formation of a heterostructure of BiVO4 and other semiconductors, such as g-C3N4 [12,13] and TiO2 [14] improves photoinduced charge separation and migration, bringing out enhanced photocatalytic performance. For an alternative method of improving photocatalytic performance, the modification of precious metal nanoparticles (NPs) (Au, Ag) on the surface of semiconductors not only facilitates the separation of photoexcited charges but also increases the efficiency of light utilization [15].

Precious metal NPs exhibit a high capacity for Vis light absorption due to LSPR effects [16,17]. Furthermore, the Schottky energy barrier at the interface between semiconductors and precious metal NPs can weaken the recombination of photoexcited charges. Therefore, the decoration of Ag-NPs on the BiVO4 surface has drawn attention to the possible fabrication of hybrid composites for photocatalytic applications. However, further improvement in the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4 combined with Ag-NPs is still required to utilize all the Vis light spectra since Ag-NPs only absorb light wavelengths below ~550 nm. Accordingly, Ag/BiVO4 composites capable of utilizing light of longer wavelengths must be developed to expand the practical applications.

One approach is to utilize all light spectra using upconverting materials. GNDs consisting of monolayers or multilayer graphene have recently garnered attention in various fields due to the superior properties induced by quantum confinement effect [18]. Nitrogen doping of carbon-based materials was presented to enhance their electron transfer capability and photocatalytic activity [19]. Thus, the use of N-GNDs as cocatalysts is based on their low toxicity, ecofriendly features, superior electron transfer capability, and photochemical properties [20]. Hybridization of semiconductor photocatalysts with N-GNDs significantly improves the photocatalytic performance of materials since N-GNDs serve as electron traps and facilitate photoinduced electron transfer. In addition, the upconversion of near-infrared (NIR) light into Vis light by N-GNDs, may facilitate the absorption of Vis light [18]. Consequently, N-GNDs/Ag-NPs-modified BiVO4 can be used for full-spectrum light utilization.

In this work, a novel N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst was prepared in an effort to enhance the Vis light response capacity of BiVO4 based on the LSPR effect of Ag-NPs and upconversion effect of N-GNDs. BiVO4 is employed as a platform to load Ag-NPs and N-GNDs. The synthesized N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 show superior photocatalytic performance for the photodegradation of TC•HCl compared with single BiVO4. The synergistic effects of Ag-NPs and N-GNDs enable a full-spectrum response to light and the rapid migration and separation of photoinduced charge carriers ensures excellent photocatalytic performance of N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4. Based on the optical and photochemical behaviors of the ternary composite photocatalyst, we suggest a possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of TC•HCl degradation over N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4. Successful fabrication of the novel N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic performance and full spectrum response may facilitate the development of highly efficient photocatalysts for light energy utilization and environmental protection.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid monohydrate, urea, ammonium vanadate (NH4VO3), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ammonium solution (NH3·H2O), nitric acid (HNO3), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), ethanol, methylene blue (MB), 1,4-benzoquione (BQ), n-butyl alcohol (NBA),disodium ethylenediamine and tetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) were commercially purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.,Ltd. All chemical reagents were analytical grade and used directly without further purification.

N-GNDs were synthesized using a solvothermal technology. In a typical synthesis, 0.105 g of citric acid monohydrate and 0.09 g of urea were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized (DI) water with vigorous stirring for 0.5 h. Next, the transparent solution was emptied into a Teflon autoclaves (20 mL) and heated at 160 °C for 5 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 36 h. Finally, an N-GNDs suspension was obtained.

BiVO4 was also synthesized using a solvothermal method. First, 2.425 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 100 mL of nitric acid (2 mol/L). Next, 0.585 g of NH4VO3 was added to the suspension and the pH was adjusted to 2.1 using NH3·H2O with vigorous stirring. After 2 h, the product was moved to a Teflon autoclaves (100 mL) and hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 24 h. A yellow powder was obtained, which was washed repeatedly with DI water and ethanol. Finally, the products were dried at 60 °C overnight.

To deposit Ag NPs on BiVO4, 0.4 g of BiVO4 were dispersed in 35 mL of DI water. Next, 0.1 g of PVP and a certain amount of AgNO3 were gradually added to the BiVO4 suspension under vigorous stirring for 2 h. The mixture was emptied into a 50 mL Teflon autoclaves and hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 3 h. Finally, the products were collected, washed, and dried in vacuum at 70 °C for 6 h.

Next, N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst was prepared by coating the prepared Ag-deposited BiVO4 (Ag/BiVO4) with N-GNDs. First, 0.2 g Ag/BiVO4 was dispersed in 10 mL of DI water. Next, 10 mL of N-GNDs suspension was gradually added to the Ag/BiVO4 suspension. The mixture was reacted by rotary evaporation at 50 °C. Finally, the obtained product was dried in vacuum at 70 °C for 6 h. A series of N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 composites with different Ag contents (2.7, 5.4, 8.1, 10.8 and 13.5 wt%) were prepared and designated as NAB-1, NAB-2, NAB-3, NAB-4, and NAB-5, respectively.

The crystalline structures of the samples were examined by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D8 Advance, Bruker) with Cu Kα radiation at a scan rate of 0.02° (2θ) s−1. The morphology and microstructure of the samples were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7001F, JEOL) with an accelerating voltage of 0.5–30 kV and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100, JEOL) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV2450 with BaSO4 as the reference sample. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were analyzed with an AdvantXP4200 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PHI5300 (Perkin Elmer) with aluminum Kα radiation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) was analyzed using a Bruker EPR A300-10/12 spectrometer. Photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were taken on a CHI 852C (Chenhua Instruments Co. Shanghai china) electrochemical workstation.

The photocatalytic performance of the samples was measured via degradation of TC•HCl in a photocatalytic reactor under Vis light irradiation using a 300 W Xe lamp. The as-synthesized photocatalysts (0.05 g). were dispersed in 100 mL of TC•HCl solution at a concentration of 20 mg/L. Prior to light irradiation, the mixed solution was stirred for 30 min in the dark to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The light was switched, and 3 mL aliquots of the test solution were obtained at predetermined time intervals and filtered for photocatalytic separation. The filtrate was analyzed with a UV- spectrometer at its maximum absorption wavelength of 357 nm. Photocatalytic degradation efficiency was calculated using the following formula:
Photodegradation
e f f i c i e n c y =
C 0
- C
C 0
× 100
. The C0 and C in the formula denote the concentrations of TC•HCl before photocatalytic reaction and during the different photocatalytic intervals, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of the as-prepared photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline and phase structures of the samples were determined via XRD analysis. The XRD patterns of pure BiVO4 and NAB-4 are displayed in Fig. 1 . A comparison of the XRD pattern (JCPDS PDF# 14-0688) of the BiVO4 standard reveals that loading of N-GNDs and Ag did not alter the crystalline phases. The XRD patterns of both pure BiVO4 and N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 represent the monoclinic phase structure of BiVO4 [21]. The NAB-4 peaks at 2θ = 38.1 and 46.1 correspond to the (111) and (200) crystal planes of cubic Ag, respectively (JCPDS PDF# 65-2871) [22]. Also, the weak diffraction peak located at 2θ = 64.5 (Fig. 1b) may correspond to the (220) crystal plane of cubic Ag. However, we could not detect any characteristic N-GNDs diffraction peaks in the NAB-4 sample due to the low concentration of N-GNDs in NAB-4 [23]. No peaks representing impurities were detected, which implies good crystallinity.

We used SEM and TEM to characterize the morphology of the as-synthesized samples. The results are displayed in Fig. 2 . Pure BiVO4 sample is irregular in shape with smooth surface (Fig. 2a). In comparison, a few NPs are dispersed on the surface of the Ag/BiVO4 and NAB-4 samples (Fig. 2b and c). As evident in from Fig. 2b and c, no significant difference was found between the SEM images of Ag/BiVO4 and NAB-4. However, the TEM images of NAB-4, which represent a rough surface, suggest that N-GNDs and Ag are distributed on the surface of BiVO4 (Fig. 2d). A few NPs can be observed on the surface of Ag-NPs under higher magnification of TEM images (Fig. 2e). In addition, the EDS spectrum of NAB-4 reveals the co-existence of C, N, V, Bi, Ag and O (Fig. 2f), the percentages of Bi, V, O, Ag, C, and N were 51.55, 12.62, 15.92, 9.63, 7.36, and 2.92 wt%, respectively (inset in Fig. 2f). These results indicate successful preparation of the N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst, which is consistent with the XRD results.

The elemental composition of pure BiVO4 and NAB-4 was assessed via XPS, XPS high-resolution spectra for C 1s, Bi 4f, V 2p and O 1s of BiVO4 and NAB-4 and for N 1s and Ag 3d of NAB-4 are detected and displayed in Fig. 3 . In the C 1s XPS core spectra of both samples (Fig. 3a), a broad peak of the NAB-4 can be deconvoluted into three peaks at around 284.6, 286.8, and 289.0 eV, which represent the C=C, C-O and O-C=O bonds, respectively [24]. Compared with C 1s spectrum of pure BiVO4, the intensities of the peak corresponding to C-O and O-C=O bonds in NAB-4 increased, which suggests that the N-GNDs were successfully decorated on the surface of the NAB-4. The Bi 4f core XPS spectrum of NAB-4 (Fig. 3b) presents two peaks at 158.9 and 164.2 eV, assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, implying that the Bi oxidation state was +3. Also, the V 2p XPS spectrum of NAB-4 (Fig. 3c) exhibits two characteristic peaks at 524.3 and 516.4 eV, identified as V 2p1/2 and V 2p3/2 of the V5+ species of BiVO4, respectively [25]. In particular, the Bi 4f and V 2p peaks of NAB-4 were shifted toward higher binding energies compared with those of pure BiVO4. The shift toward higher binding energy indicates a strong chemical interaction between BiVO4 and N-GNDs, facilitating the transfer of photoinduced electrons from BiVO4 to N-GNDs [26]. The XPS spectrum of O 1s in Fig. 3d can be deconvoluted into a pair of peaks around 529.8 and 533.8 eV, representing the lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen, respectively [27]. The peak in the N 1s XPS spectrum of NAB-4 (Fig. 3e) corresponds to two peaks centered at 398.1 and 401.9 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N and graphitic N, respectively [28]. The N 1s signal indicates successful doping of N into the GNDs in the NAB-4 composite. In addition, the Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 3f) presents two main peaks at 367.7 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 373.8 eV (Ag 3d3/2), which confirms the existence of metallic Ag (Ag°) [29]. Consequently, the elemental XPS core spectra of NAB-4 suggest successful synthesis of N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of TC•HCl using the synthesized photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation of hazardous pollutants is a common method used to estimate the activity of photocatalysts. Thus, the photocatalytic degradation of TC•HCl was investigated, as shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a suggests that the photocatalytic performance of both Ag/BiVO4 and N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 was significantly enhanced compared with that of pure BiVO4. In comparison, we also evaluated the initial adsorption characteristics of TC•HCl on the samples in dark. All the samples exhibited weak adsorption less than ~8% within 30 min (Fig. S1). The degradation efficiency of TC•HCl was particularly high over N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4, which contains N-GNDs. Accordingly, the photocatalytic capacity of BiVO4 was gradually improved via the stepwise introduction of Ag-NPs and N-GNDs. The introduction of N-GNDs into photocatalysts improves the efficiency of light absorption and facilitates the separation efficiency of photoexcited carriers [30]. Therefore, N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 photocatalyst exhibits better photocatalytic activity than Ag/BiVO4 without GQDs.

Fig. 4b shows the dynamic curves of TC•HCl photodegradation over the NAB-1, -2, -3, -4, and -5 samples with different Ag concentrations. The photocatalytic efficiency gradually improved with the increase in Ag content in N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 (NAB-1 to NAB-4). The NAB-4 sample displayed the highest photocatalytic performance with a degradation efficiency of 85.4% within 80 min. Nevertheless, the degradation efficiency declined when the Ag content exceeded 13.5 wt% (NAB-5). Excessive deposition of Ag can lead to further agglomeration of Ag particles during the synthesis of Ag/BiVO4 composite. Agglomeration reduces the number of active sites on N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4, and slightly decreased its photocatalytic efficiency in the presence of high Ag content. A similar tendency of the composite photocatalyst with varying composite ratio has been reported previously [31]. In summary, our composite BiVO4 with Ag-NPs and N-GNDs (NAB-4) exhibits satisfactory catalytic efficiency during the photodegradation of TC•HCl, compared with related composite photocatalysts reported previously (table S1).

The temporal curve photodegradation reaction is fitted by a pseudo-first order kinetics equation, and the corresponding slope of the fitting line indicates the rate constant [32]. As displayed in Fig. 4c, the rate constants of BiVO4, NAB-1, NAB-2, NAB-3, NAB-4 and NAB-5 are 0.0037, 0.00893, 0.01535, 0.01686, 0.02433 and 0.02071 min−1, respectively. NAB-4 presents the highest reaction rate constant for TC•HCl degradation, which is nearly 6.6-fold higher that of pure BiVO4. Fig. 4d shows the absorbance spectra of TC•HCl solutions over N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4, under 80 min of light irradiation. The main absorption peaks of TC•HCl almost disappear after 80 min of photodegradation, which indirectly indicates the rupture of most of the TC•HCl molecules into small molecules or ions.

Stability is a very important factor determining the performance of a photocatalyst. Thus, we carried out cyclic TC•HCl photodegradation experiments with the NAB-4 sample (Fig. 5 a). An TC•HCl degradation rate of >85% was maintained even after five runs. Besides, the XRD patterns of NBA-4 after five successive runs did not alter the crystalline structure (Fig. 5b). These results confirm that the NAB-4 composite photocatalyst is reusable and stable.

Photocatalytic activity is mainly attributed to light absorption capability and the separation and transfer efficiency of photoexcited carriers in a photocatalyst. Herein, we investigated the roles of each of these factors in BiVO4 composites modified with Ag particles and N-GNDs. Fig. 6 a presents the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as-synthesized pure BiVO4, Ag/BiVO4, and NAB-4. An absorption edge for pure BiVO4 appears at ~520 nm. However, Ag/BiVO4 exhibits enhanced absorption intensity at Vis and NIR wavelengths. Extension of light absorption to the longer wavelength region is attributed to the LSPR effects of Ag particles in the composite [33]. When N-GNDs were adhibited to Ag/BiVO4 (NAB-4), the broad-spectrum response was further enhanced in the Vis and NIR regions, which may be due to upconversion of NIR to Vis light by N-GNDs [34]. Hence, the light absorption of BiVO4 in the Vis and NIR regions was significantly improved by modifying BiVO4 with Ag particles and N-GNDs due to the synergetic effects of LSPR and light upconversion. Moreover, the band gap energy (Eg) of BiVO4 was ~2.38 eV and was calculated using the equation, αhν = A(hν − Eg)2 , and the plot of
( α h v )
2
vs.
hv
, as displayed in Fig. 6b. The valence band maximum (EVB) and conduction band minimum (ECB) of a semiconductor can be calculated via the following two equations:
E
CB
= X -
E e
- 0.5
E g
and
E
VB
=
E g
+
E
CB
in which Ee is the energy of a free electron on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5 eV) and X denotes the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor [35]. Thus, the calculated ECB and EVB values for BiVO4 were 0.47 and 2.85 eV, respectively.

The charge transfer behavior of photogenerated carriers is another important determinant of photocatalytic activity. The transient photocurrent response indirectly reveals the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers [36]. The transient photocurrent spectra obviously demonstrate that the photocurrent intensity of NAB-4 was substantially higher than that of Ag/BiVO4 and BiVO4 under Vis light (Fig. 7 a), suggesting enhanced separation efficiency of photoexcited carriers in the NAB-4 composite. Moreover, NAB-4 has a smaller arc radius than Ag/BiVO4 and BiVO4 in the EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 7b). The small radius implies low charge transfer resistance in NAB-4.

Effective separation of photoinduced carriers enables photocatalytic reactions. The photoluminescence (PL) value can also be used to evaluate the recombination rate of photoexcited carriers in a photocatalytic system [37]. Thus, the PL spectra of the samples with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm were measured (Fig. 8 a). The PL emission intensity of NAB-4 was lower than that of pure BiVO4 and Ag/BiVO4, revealing a sharp decrease in charge carriers recombination in NAB. Furthermore, the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of BiVO4, Ag/BiVO4 and NAB-4 are displayed in Fig. 8b. The fluorescent lifetime (0.57 ns) of NAB-4 is shorter than those of pure BiVO4 (0.74 ns) and Ag/BiVO4 (0.60 ns). The short lifetime is attributed to the opening of an additional nonradioactive decay channel via carrier recombination between BiVO4 and Ag and carrier transfer from Ag and BiVO4 to N-GNDs. This result is in line with the PL results [38]. Together, these results imply that Ag-NPs and N-GNDs act as electron acceptors that accelerate carrier migration, which improves the degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst. Therefore, all photoelectrochemical and photoluminescent measurements demonstrate that NAB-4 has a lower likelihood of recombination and a faster photogenerated carrier transfer rate than Ag/BiVO4 and BiVO4 alone. Consequently, NAB-4 exhibits improved photocatalytic performance via enhanced migration and separation efficiency of photoexcited carriers.

Active species capture experiments were performed on NAB-4 to identify the reactive species during the photodegradation (Fig. 9 ). We employed EDTA-2Na, IPA and BQ as holes,
OH and
O2 – scavengers, respectively. The degradation efficiency of NAB-4 with EDTA-2Na and BQ decreased significantly in the trapping experiments (Fig. 9a), demonstrating that holes are crucial and
O2 – plays a role in the photodegradation. However, no apparent decline in the photodegradation efficiency of NAB-4 was detected after the addition of IPA, which confirms that
OH is not a key reactive species. To further elucidate the active species, ESR was implemented to detect
OH and
O2 – generation in the presence of NAB-4. As displayed in Fig. 9b and c, no signal appeared in the dark while DMPO-
O2 – and
OH signals were readily detected under Vis light. The results imply that both
O2 – and
OH are produced during photocatalytic degradation, which is consistent with the results of the trapping experiments [39].

Based on the experimental results discussed above, the possible mechanism of enhanced light absorption behavior and photocatalytic degradation of TC•HCl over N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 could be proposed, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 12 . When N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 is irradiated by sunlight, NIR light is absorbed by N-GNDs and light of a shorter wavelength is emitted by the N-GNDs owing to their capacity for light upconversion effect. The converted light excites BiVO4 to generate photoexcited electrons and holes. Meanwhile, BiVO4 is directly excited by Vis light to yield photoexcited carriers. The photoexcited electrons on the ECB of BiVO4 can not reduce the O2 to
O2 – since the potential of O2/
O2 – (−0.33 eV, NHE) is more negative compared with the ECB of BiVO4 (+0.47 eV, NHE) [41]. Thus, the photoexcited electrons in the ECB of BiVO4 rapidly migrate to N-GNDs and Ag-NPs because of matched band alignment [42]. Also, the Schottky energy barrier between Ag-NPs and BiVO4 improves directional transport of photoexcited electrons from BiVO4 to Ag-NPs. The transported electrons gain energy from the electric field (LSPR effect) near Ag-NPs and reduce O2 to
O2 –. The N-GNDs act as electron acceptors to capture photoexcited electrons from BiVO4 and hot electrons from Ag-NPs (LSPR effect), which also reduces the O2 to
O2 – [43].
O2 – oxidizes TC•HCl to small molecules.

Meanwhile, photoinduced holes on the EVB of BiVO4 may directly partake in the degradation of TC•HCl and oxidize water molecule to
OH as the EVB of BiVO4 (+2.85 eV, NHE) is more positive than the potential of H2O/
OH (+2.4 eV, NHE) [44]. Eventually, the interactions between holes,
O2 – and
OH result in degradation of TC•HCl into small molecules. Consequently, co-modification with both N-GNDs and Ag-NPs enhances the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4 in broad-spectrum solar light.

Herein, we report the construction of a novel N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst developed by co-deposition of N-GNDs and Ag-NPs on the BiVO4 surface. The photocatalyst displays optimum photocatalytic efficiency for degrading TC•HCl. When the loading amounts of Ag is 10.8 wt%, the degradation efficiency of the as-produced photocatalyst is approximately 7.2-fold greater than that of pure BiVO4 within 80 min. The improved photocatalytic performance is attributed to the synergistic effects of N-GNDs and Ag-NPs. The LSPR effect of Ag-NPs and the upconverted effect of N-GNDs synergistically improved the photoresponse, leading to an abundance of photoinduced carriers. In addition, N-GNDs and Ag-NPs as electron acceptors effectively inhibit the recombination of photoexcited carriers. In trapping experiments,
O2 – and h+ are the main active substance in the degradation process. The novel N-GNDs/Ag/BiVO4 ternary photocatalyst may represent a strategy for the fabrication of broad-spectrum photocatalysts.

Changchang Ma: Writing - original draft. Syed Taj Ud Din: Formal analysis. Won Cheol Seo: Visualization. Jeongwoo Lee: Methodology. Youjoong Kim: Data curation. Hyun Jung: Writing - review & editing. Woochul Yang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908080), the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MIST) (No. 2020R1C1C1011667, No. 2019R1A2C1002844, and 2016R1A6A1A03012877), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20180884).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.118423.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
